questions.content: The aim of genetic engineering is to remove a gene from one organism and transfer it into another so that the gene is _____ .
questions.explanation: The text states that 'The aim of genetic engineering is to remove a gene (or genes) from one organism and transfer it into another so that the gene is expressed.'

questions.content: DNA that contains lengths of nucleotides from two different organisms is called _____ DNA.
questions.explanation: According to the text, recombinant DNA is 'the DNA that has been altered by this process and which now contains lengths of nucleotides from two different organisms.'

questions.content: In forensic DNA analysis, DNA is extracted from _____ blood cells.
questions.explanation: The text specifically states: 'The DNA is extracted from white blood cells.'

questions.content: To extract DNA from cells, the forensic scientist first treats the cells with a reagent that causes them to _____ open.
questions.explanation: The text describes the DNA extraction process: 'To extract the DNA, the forensic scientist first treats the cells with a reagent that causes them to break open.'

questions.content: When a DNA sample is too small to analyze, the quantity is increased using the (_____).
questions.explanation: The text explains that 'It is likely that the sample of DNA will be too small to analyse, so the quantity is increased by using DNA polymerase to replicate key regions of the DNA' using PCR.

questions.content: Examples of vectors used in genetic engineering include plasmids, viruses and _____ .
questions.explanation: The text lists examples of vectors as 'plasmids, viruses and liposomes' which deliver the gene to the cells of the organism.

questions.content: Unlike selective breeding where whole sets of genes are involved, genetic engineering often results in the transfer of a single _____ .
questions.explanation: The text states: 'Unlike selective breeding, where whole sets of genes are involved, genetic engineering often results in the transfer of a single gene.'

questions.content: During DNA extraction, a crude sample of DNA is captured on magnetic _____ , leaving most of the cell debris and protein behind.
questions.explanation: The text describes: 'A crude sample of DNA is captured on magnetic beads, leaving most of the cell debris and protein behind in solution.'

questions.content: It is quite likely that your DNA is _____ , which makes it useful for identification in forensic analysis.
questions.explanation: The text mentions: 'It is quite likely that your DNA is unique' and explains how this uniqueness allows for positive identification through DNA profiling.

questions.content: The organism which expresses new genes from another organism is known as a transgenic organism or a (_____).
questions.explanation: The text defines: 'The organism which now expresses the new gene or genes is known as a transgenic organism or a genetically modified organism (GMO).'
